speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is December 7, 1995. And Gerda, we left off on the last tape. You were in Landeshut.

speaker: interviewee
text: Landeshut, right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You asked about a boys' camp in Landeshut. And one of the young men who came to Landeshut, as a matter of fact, on his own volition very foolishly, was a young man by the name of Abek Feigenblatt. He was sort of a boyfriend, and I think he thought of the relationship much more seriously in a way than I did. He was about six years older than I. As a matter of fact, he made an incredible gesture. His family did when I was in Sosnowitz. They wanted to buy me out of [NON-ENGLISH] to be with his family. I-- so I was still very naive. I must have intuitively known that if I accepted that, that I was making a commitment, and I was-- certainly did not want to make any type of a commitment. Again, I must quote my father. And I don't know if it was really in the relationship with that, but my father-- we had long, long discussions with my father and I. And my father always said, you know, it was a terrible time, certainly abnormal times. Don't ever make any decisions during the war. And I don't know if it was in relation to that and that which would apply to normal times. I had very romantic ideas about any future relationships that I would want. I liked Abek a great deal, but much more like a friend or a brother. But I was in no way romantically involved with him. However, I am very grateful for the things which he did for me. He sent me food to camp. He sent me clothing and things which I desperately needed. And he volunteered to come to, to Landeshut. And I saw him in, in Landeshut a number of times, never alone, except for one time, thanks to Mrs. Kugler. Conditions in Landeshut were very bad, but I was, I was able to give him some food. Ilse and I managed that. Ilse was, was truly like a sister and did everything for me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I am still deeply indebted to him and to his family for the type of sacrifices they were willing to bring for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were leaving Landeshut on my 20th birthday, which was the 8th of May, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At that point, of course, it was destination unknown. But we came into a camp called Grunberg in Schleisen, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Grunberg was the most cruel camp of them all. Physically, exquisitely beautiful. The most renowned roses grown and many of the German things were there. I don't know how many hundreds of varieties of roses and vineyards and everything. And it seemed to be sort of in direct contrast and irony to, to the misery of the camp. There were 1,000 girls there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were housed in, in a huge factory hall with, I think, three or four bunk beds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were told that the worst work in Grunberg was to be sent to [NON-ENGLISH], which was spinning factories, because a lot of girls contracted tuberculosis. And I don't remember, every six or eight weeks, girls were X-rayed. And if you showed signs of it, you were shipped to Auschwitz. But very soon, selection was made, and I was selected to go to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my friend Lisl and my friend Susie. Not Ilse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very hard work, you know. You had to try to tie the threads, sort of knit. The machines opened, went back and forth, and the thread would break, and you, you had to have a lot of dexterity to, to tie that. If not, you could get your fingers crossed in the machine. And of course you had to run up and down all day, all night. It was extremely difficult work. But somehow we managed it, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I witnessed some very brutal beatings in, in, in Grunberg. There was one person that was a sadist. He had this signet ring, and he would beat a victim until, until he was bloody. And things were not pretty there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called him Lalusz. What his name was, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it, I knew it at one, at one, I knew it at one time, but I don't recall it anymore. In, in Bolkenhain there was a [NON-ENGLISH], whom we feared, was a, a miserable creature. And the director there was name was Keller. But this one escapes me. I really don't remember. I knew his name at one point. I don't remember it any longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would rather not discuss it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We, we went to X-rays every so often. And it was at that point that you knew that you got the lease on life for another six or eight weeks or what have you. And there were some very heartbreaking farewells of the girls that were sent to Auschwitz. I had a very good friend there-- her name was Hanka Keller-- who worked in the, in the kitchen. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she occasionally gave me a bowl of food, for which I was deeply grateful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] But one day luck smiled at me there. I was chosen, my friend Susie and I were chosen, to weigh crates of, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of the type of-- I don't know what to call it. Threads, perhaps, for the want of a, of a, a a better word. They were extremely heavy, and you had to take it off a conveyor belt immediately and then put it on the belt back properly. What could happen if you didn't-- because the thing was marching through the entire factory, and if it wasn't put on properly, it could cause some of the things to, to have some accident. And of course, you know, all those things were, were punishable very heavily because it was sabotage. Anything that you did, it was sabotage. In any event, if you've got this coveted work, you got an extra bowl of food a day. And my friend, my best friend-- we were three friends, Ilse, Susie, with whom I made a bet on the train to camp for strawberries and cream payable after the war. I said the war would be over in six months, and she said we are not going to survive. And Lisl. We were the four of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it meant that Ilse and I, because, you know, I was getting some extra food, and Susie was getting the extra food, and Lisl would have it that we are going to have more food. That was cause for great celebration.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In Grunberg also, we witnessed-- because daily or perhaps every second day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] transport of clothing would come, apparently from Auschwitz or perhaps from other camps. And the clothing was torn into bits by something called a [NON-ENGLISH]. And those bits then were recycled to the type of threads, the weaving material. And I used to walk by it in the night, because we worked mostly on the night shift, and saw that. And sometimes I thought that I spotted my mother's coat. But then I thought a lot of people wear black coats. And it was usually in those nights that I used to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] paint my homecoming, which was sort of a picture that, that helped one survive. And those pictures usually surfaced in the darkest moments. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I always imagined how I would come home last. And sometimes I thought I would come home during the night, and I would not wake them. But then I knew I'd be much too impetuous, and I probably would. And my mother would appear at the doorway in her black housecoat with yellow flowers, which I particularly liked on her, even though I knew that we had long sold it for a loaf of bread and a pound of margarine. But with the miracle of the homecoming, it all would be there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I will appear, and my father would be there, then my mother would say go wake up Arthur. You know he never gets up on time. And I would run up. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that sort of dispelled the, the noise of the [NON-ENGLISH], which was tearing the cloth from Auschwitz. We're going home. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So Grunberg was a temple of, of dreams and of nightmares. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My friend Lisl and I had a wonderful way of when we worked the night shift-- her bunk was adjacent to mine, and we played sort of a game. And she would say, what are you doing today? You know, I'd say I go to the garden and gather some apples. And I'm not going to go to school today, it's such and such. And we, we had perfected that game.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then things got tougher. And there was very little raw material, which we rejoiced because we knew that apparently, the Germans are experiencing harder time. But we also knew it would be a race now. Are they going to kill us or will they let us survive? We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And several times the electricity stopped and not too many more announcements of victories. And one day [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was snowing very heavily. We were all herded into one enormous hall. It was a great deal of commotion in the adjacent hall. We talked to each other through the doors or the cracks or whatever and we realized that 2,000 girls came from Auschwitz. And they told us that the front was closed, that the Russians were taking Auschwitz, and they were evacuated and that they were marching and that they had many-- many girls had died on the way. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The following day we were given a double or perhaps even triple portion of bread. And I again, in true fashion, had a terrible cold. I was running a temperature. I was coughing. And my friends Susie and Lisl and Ilse were very concerned. They said, you know, how are you going to-- how was I was going to be? You're feeling so sick, and if you could only pull through.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the morning, very early, the gates opened to an incredible picture of, of snow drifts, of, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of bitter cold. And we were-- two-- we were put into two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different columns, 2,000 girls ours, 2,000 of the other girls. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And four abreast. Susie, Ilse, Lisl and I. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the SS lifted their whips. It was forward march. And we started to march. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We left a bloody trail in, in the snow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were executions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some people simply couldn't go on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The nightmare of the death march started [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 29th of January, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frau Kugler was no longer with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We saw her last in Landeshut. She was not in Grunberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember in Landeshut she was supposed to, when we left there, she was supposed to search our bundles, but she didn't. She embraced us, and she was crying. And I remember what she did. She said to me, 'Don't forget me.' I didn't want to forget her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the horror of, of, of that started. We were-- sometimes we slept in a barn. Sometimes we were outside. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Sometimes we got something to eat. One time for four days we got nothing to eat. We were eating snow. Eating snow is a horrible thing. The more you eat, the, the more thirsty you are. And of course, diarrhea and all those things. It, it was pretty brutal. And we marched from the 29th of January 1945 until the 6th of May. Where were you marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our original orders apparently were to be sent to Ravensbruck. Ravensbruck was a murder camp near Berlin. But Ravensbruck was taken by the Allies, and we were sort of zig-zagging through Germany and through Dresden, through [NON-ENGLISH] and to later retrace that march. We finally-- we were running away from the front. And we finally ended up in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What guards were with you? Were they SS guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS was with us, the women SS, who were even worse than the men. They were absolutely brutal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to run away once. Ilse and I had devised a game-- not a game, a plan-- because we saw on the road, we saw refugees in horse and buggies that spoke a similar German that we did. And we felt that this would be a good time to do it. We both had German names, Ilse and Gerda, you couldn't get it better. And we sort of devised that if we are going to be separated and questioned that we had to, you know, that our mother and, and little sisters were in one of the wagons. That's where they had the identification. We would take off our, our stars. And you know, we-- that our father was in the army. And we devised a number which would be her house number and my house number and, and our telephone number. And between that we both had the same numbers, that we had the same stories, and that we would go to the police, which of course was the most horrible thing to report, that we're looking for our mothers and so on. And on the next break in one of the forests [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we pretended that we are going to go and relieve ourselves and disappear in the woods. And we would wait in the woods for a day or two and then try to go back. And when the time came I sort of gave her a signal that we should disappear now. And then suddenly she looked at me and she said, I'm afraid. Now, I, I always overruled her. I was always, you know, much more positive and [INAUDIBLE]. And even though it was really on the tip of my tongue to say, let's-- I said, well, maybe next, during the next stop. Pretty soon we heard horrible screams. 14 girls had tried to do the same thing that we did, and they were executed in front of us. And I said, I'm never going to try to run away. I'll go, I'll go on, whatever the end is going to be. Well, unfortunately for Ilse, it was a very bitter end. Ilse died a week before the war was over. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She died in my arms. Or rather I should say we went sort of to sleep, and I woke up, and she didn't. Before she died, she said she was not angry at anyone. And said no one was-- she hoped no one was angry at her. And she asked me to go on for one more week. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She also told me that if her parents and her little sister, Kitty, survived, not to tell them how she died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then she said, you have always been lucky, and you're going to be lucky. Then she started feeling sorry for me. She said, you're going to be alone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The 29th of April. And exactly a week later, maybe to the very hour of her death, we were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated in a little place called Volary in Czechoslovakia. The march came to a halt there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My friend Hanka Keller, who gave me some food as I told you, was told by one of the SS women to take off my boots. I still had my skiing shoes. And she pushed me behind someone, and she said, don't let her see you. She did not take them off. It was also that she brought three potatoes when Ilse was dying. Ilse said she was not hungry and that was one of the most incredible things not to be hungry. But Hanka brought those potatoes, and I said to her, I hope that one day I'll be able to give you a diamond for the potatoes. And I did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She lives in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the scene when Ilse died, it sort of stopped for me. You know, I was still able to pray. I always prayed because I always had something to be grateful for. I was taught to pray by thanking God for the blessings. When Ilse died, I stopped praying. I couldn't anymore. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] The march went on, and we stopped in Volary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We pretty much knew-- I think we knew that it was pretty much up, that the truck was coming to take some of the girls. And my friend Hanka said, get, you know, get on the truck because they shouldn't see, shouldn't see your boots. And I said, you know, I, I am in no hurry. I'll just sit here and wait for the next truck or whatever's going to happen next. And then sort of a-- some silence and quiet. I, I think I was very ill at that point, you know. Ilse died of typhoid, and I probably had caught it. And I knew I was running a fever. And quite honestly, at this point, I really didn't care too much anymore. But I knew that this would be-- if we survived another day or so, it's probably going to be over. And I allowed myself the luxury of thinking of home, of my parents. I thought of my garden. And I thought if they kill me, that's going to be my memory now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and they, they came back, and there was an abandoned factory. They locked us into the factory, and they attached a time bomb. And we pretty much knew it's up for them, and it's up for us. And I remember just saying, my God, to have gone for so long and, and not to make it because it's going to be over now. Now it's going to be over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it started to rain, torrential rain. The rain obviously resulted in mud. The bomb and timer did not connect. Never went off. But they came back. There was shooting. I crawled into one of those cylinders that lay on the floor, which I saw when I went back four years ago. It's still lying there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But obviously, I was not killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And back in the factory, the factory [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the doors were thrust open. We heard voices calling in Czech, if anyone is there, get out. The war is over. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My clearest recollections, there were two Americans at night. But my clearest recollection is that of the morning. The SS had departed. They left their great coats, put on civilian clothes. I stood in the doorway of that factory. I knew I was free. But somehow the reality of it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I prayed for it for six years in every waking hour. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't-- I couldn't absorb the wonder of, of perhaps freedom until the crushing, almost overwhelming joy by seeing a strange car coming down a hill. No longer green. The white star of the American army on its hood, and two men in unfamiliar uniforms sat in it. I stood in the doorway. And one of them-- we gathered them to be Americans-- jumped out, and he came toward me. Or perhaps I should say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that just before that, I asked Lisl where Susie was, and she said she went outside to get some water, and she hadn't come back from the pump outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went out, and I found Susie. And she was lying near the pump. And I thought that she had fainted. And I shook her, and I said, Susie, the war is over. And she didn't respond. And I realized that she was dead. And I couldn't even pay my debt of the quart of strawberries and cream that we made three years before. I lost the bet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I didn't tell Lisl that I found her. I couldn't tell her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, as I mentioned, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of, of those men that sat in that Jeep came toward me. And I was looking at him with incredible awe and disbelief that I was looking at someone who fought for us. Of course, I was terribly frightened, what, you know, what his reaction would be if I tell him we are Jewish. But I felt I had to tell him. So I looked at him and I said, we are Jewish, you know. He didn't answer me, which seemed to me eternity. And then he said, so am I. This was the greatest moment of my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he asked an incredible question. He asked if he could see the other ladies, obviously a form of address we hadn't known and heard. And I told him that most of the girls were inside. They were too ill to walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He asked me to come with him. I didn't know what he meant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He held the door open for me and let me proceed him and restored me to humanity again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he has been holding the door open for me now for 50 years, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kurt Klein. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Well, pretty soon, trucks came, Red Cross trucks. They took us into a, makeshift hospital. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had the first bath in four years, five years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- all my clothes were gathered to be burnt. I went into the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lining of my boots. I had two things there. One, some poison which I had bought in one of the camps with the last piece of jewelry that was sewn into my clothes. It was against the day when we would be put into a camp to apparently amuse Germans. And I knew that, that I didn't need to keep my promise to my father. I'm going to kill myself first. I let it gladly go to destruction. And the other thing was a little bundle with the pictures of my parents and my brother and Abek, which I carried in my boot. And that, of course, I saved. And I was given a bath [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and a red, not red, a blue-and-white checkered shirt, a men's shirt, which was sort of like a nightgown, and taken to bunks with sheets, the first time I slept in sheets for years. And the nurse came and carried a tray with warm milk. And one of the cups had a flower on it. And I desperately wanted that cup with the flower. And I got it. And I started to drink the warm milk, lying in bed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is when I started to cry. And I cried for days. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] And I wondered what happened to this first young American to whom I gave my name, but I didn't know his name. And I realized that the war was still going on for a day or two, and I wondered if he was-- if something happened to him. And strangely enough, that night I started to pray again. And I prayed for his safety, not realizing that I was praying for the safety of my husband. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And I was in the hospital, and I kept asking for Lisl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I wasn't told. I was told she was very ill and that I was very ill, and I couldn't see her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some time later, I found out that Lisl had died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So of the four of us, I was the only one left.
